title: Acute Bacterial Meningitis
chief_complaint: Fever, severe headache, stiff neck for 12 hours. Now he's confused. He's a college student living in a dormitory. He missed his meningitis vaccine.

physical_exam.Neck: Severe nuchal rigidity. Positive Kernig and Brudzinski signs.
physical_exam.Skin: Petechial rash on trunk and lower extremities (non-blanching).
physical_exam.Neurologic: Confused. Papilledema absent. No focal deficits.

hidden_data.lp: Opening pressure 35 cmH2O (elevated). CSF: WBC 2,500/µL (95% neutrophils). Glucose 20 mg/dL (low). Protein 250 mg/dL (high). Gram stain: Gram-negative diplococci.
hidden_data.ct_head: No mass. No shift. Mild cerebral edema. Safe for LP.

correct_diagnosis: Acute Bacterial Meningitis (Neisseria meningitidis) with Early DIC
key_learning_points: Classic triad: Fever + Headache + Neck stiffness. Petechial/purpuric rash = meningococcemia until proven otherwise
LP contraindications: Papilledema, focal neuro deficits, GCS <8, seizure, immunocompromised (get CT first). NEVER delay antibiotics for LP
CSF: Neutrophilic pleocytosis, low glucose, high protein, positive Gram stain. Bacterial meningitis is a medical emergency
Empiric antibiotics: Ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h + Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg IV q8-12h + Dexamethasone 10mg IV q6h (before or with first antibiotic dose)
Dexamethasone reduces mortality and hearing loss in S. pneumoniae and Hib meningitis. Continue only if culture confirms these organisms
Prophylaxis for close contacts: Rifampin 600mg BID ×2 days OR Ciprofloxacin 500mg single dose. Notify public health
